mnemonics.title: JFC Structure
mnemonics.content: JFC = Swing + AWT + 2D + Drag & Drop + Input Methods - Remember: JFCk (Sounds like 'chicken') stands for Java Foundation Classes, kid!

mnemonics.title: EDT Rules
mnemonics.content: EDT = Event Dispatch Thread - Remember: 'Every Deventually Trequires EDT' - All GUI events eventually require the EDT for processing

acronyms.usage: The single thread that handles all GUI events and updates in Swing applications
A set of libraries for building GUIs, including Swing, AWT, 2D, and Drag & Drop
Design pattern where Model stores data, View handles display, Controller handles user input
Swing's architecture allowing runtime changes to visual appearance
ComponentUI subclass responsible for rendering Swing components
Java's original GUI toolkit, replaced largely by Swing for most applications

visualTips.visualization: Draw an inverted tree: Object → Component → Container → JComponent → AbstractButton → JButton. This shows the inheritance chain for button components.
Think of JRootPane as a sandwich: Top layer (Glass Pane) → Middle layer (Content Pane + Menu Bar) → Bottom layer (Layered Pane). Glass pane catches everything first.
Remember the paint() waterfall: paintComponent (draws you) → paintBorder (draws your outline) → paintChildren (draws your kids). Never call these directly!

analogies.analogy: Think of heavyweight components as old-fashioned picture frames (physical, substantial) that always sit on top. Lightweight components are like transparencies that can stack but might get hidden.
The EDT is like a single checkout line at a store - only one person (task) can be served at a time. If someone takes too long, everyone waits.
PLAF is like changing clothes - you can swap the entire outfit (appearance) without changing who you are (functionality). Same components, different look.
Double buffering is like painting on a canvas before putting it on the wall - you complete the whole picture off-screen, then display it instantly to avoid flicker.
